coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of high cycling stability flexible micro-supercapacitor using electrodeposited polyaniline-nitrogen sulphur doped carbon dot binary nanocomposites
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Architha, M
Neena, P K
Navaneeth, Punnakkal
Satheesh Babu, T G
Suneesh, Punathil Vasu
coredata.dc:description: Micro-supercapacitors fabricated using polyaniline are ideal for the energy requirements in flexible and wearable electronic devices. Their drawback of low cycling stability due to swelling can be minimised using nanocomposites of polyaniline. This work aims to fabricate a micro-supercapacitor using electrodeposited polyaniline-carbon dot nanocomposite and validate its capacitive performance. Nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon dots synthesised via the hydrothermal method showed a high quantum efficiency of 70.02 % with respect to quinine sulphate in sulfuric acid medium. Electrodeposited PANI/N-S doped carbon dot binary nanocomposite showed an areal capacitance of 990 mF cm-2, which is much higher compared to pristine PANI with 754 mF cm-2. Two-electrode symmetric micro-supercapacitor fabricated using PVA-H2SO4 gel electrolyte showed an areal capacitance of 276.9 mF cm-2 at 0.4 mA cm-2 with a high energy density of 24.6 µWh cm-2 and a power density of 0.17 mW cm-2. In addition to this, the fabricated device exhibited excellent cycling stability with 98.49 % capacitance retention even after 15000 cycles. The device also showed 93.3 % capacitance retention even after 2000 bending cycles. Thus, the fabricated micro-supercapacitor can contribute to flexible as well as portable electronic applications, as the need for miniaturized energy storage devices is in high demand.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrodeposited polyaniline
N-S doped carbon dot
Flexible supercapacitor
Screen printing
Micro-supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of N-S doped carbon dots (CDs)
Electrochemical synthesis of polyaniline nanocomposite (PANI/N-S CDs)
Spectroscopic characterisation of N-S doped carbon dots
Electrochemical characterisation of PANI/N-S doped nanocomposite
Testing of two-electrode assembly symmetric supercapacitor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: India Ministry of Science & Technology Department of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India, through the project (DST/CEST/AMEST/2022/55). The authors express their sincere thanks to Amrita School of Physical Sciences, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham for Raman spectrophotometer facility and SAIF, CUSAT for TEM facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CVs obtained on the (A) PANI modified electrode at different scan rates, (B) electrodeposited PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite with various compositions of CDs, and (C) electrodeposited PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite obtained with 50 µL of CDs at different scan rates, in 1 M H2SO4. (B inset) Variation in areal capacitance of electrodeposited PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite with composition of N-S doped CDs. (D) Comparison of the areal capacitance of PANI and PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite at various scan rates, (E) GCD of the optimised electrodeposited nanocomposite, in 1 M H2SO4, Nyquist (F) and Bode F(inset) plots of PANI and PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite in 1 M H2SO4.

Percentage current contribution of capacitive and diffusion-controlled processes at increasing scan rates for (A) electrodeposited PANI and (B) electrodeposited PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite, tested in 1 M H2SO4. Linear plot showing log scan rate vs log peak current for (C) electrodeposited PANI and (D) electrodeposited PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite, tested in 1 M H2SO4 .
(A) TEM image and (A inset) HR-TEM image recorded for N-S doped CDs,(B) Particle size distribution histogram for N-S doped CDs, FESEM images of (C) PANI and (D) PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite.
(A) GIXRD and (B) Raman spectroscopic analysis of pure PANI and PAN/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite, Deconvoluted XPS spectra of (C) O1s (D) C1s (E) N1s (F) S2p respectively.

CVs showing (A) comparison of the electrodeposited PANI and PANI/N-S CDs nanocomposite-based supercapacitor, (C) scan rate study of the fabricated micro-supercapacitor, in 1 M PVA/H2SO4 gel electrolyte. GCD showing (B) comparison of electrodeposited PANI and PANI/N-S CDs nanocomposite-based supercapacitor, (E) the fabricated micro-supercapacitor tested at varying current densities, in 1 M PVA/H2SO4 gel electrolyte. (D) Bar graph representation of the areal capacitance vs. scan rate of the fabricated micro-supercapacitor (F) Nyquist plot of the fabricated micro-supercapacitor, in 1 M PVA/H2SO4 gel electrolyte.

(A) Ragone plot showing the comparison of the present work with other recently published micro-supercapacitor studies [18,22,44–50], (B) Comparison between cycling stability of the PANI and PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite-based supercapacitor showing 1500 and 15000 charge-discharge cycles, (C) Plot showing % capacitance retention vs. number of bending cycles, CVs of single, two and three cells of the fabricated micro-supercapacitor connected in (D) series and (E) parallel, (F) Series combination of the fabricated micro-supercapacitor for suitable application studies.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of high cycling stability flexible micro-supercapacitor using electrodeposited polyaniline-nitrogen sulphur doped carbon dot binary nanocomposites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Micro-supercapacitors fabricated using polyaniline are ideal for the energy requirements in flexible and wearable electronic devices. Their drawback of low cycling stability due to swelling can be minimised using nanocomposites of polyaniline. This work aims to fabricate a micro-supercapacitor using electrodeposited polyaniline-carbon dot nanocomposite and validate its capacitive performance. Nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon dots synthesised via the hydrothermal method showed a high quantum efficiency of 70.02 % with respect to quinine sulphate in sulfuric acid medium. Electrodeposited PANI/N-S doped carbon dot binary nanocomposite showed an areal capacitance of 990 mF cm-2, which is much higher compared to pristine PANI with 754 mF cm-2. Two-electrode symmetric micro-supercapacitor fabricated using PVA-H2SO4 gel electrolyte showed an areal capacitance of 276.9 mF cm-2 at 0.4 mA cm-2 with a high energy density of 24.6 µWh cm-2 and a power density of 0.17 mW cm-2. In addition to this, the fabricated device exhibited excellent cycling stability with 98.49 % capacitance retention even after 15000 cycles. The device also showed 93.3 % capacitance retention even after 2000 bending cycles. Thus, the fabricated micro-supercapacitor can contribute to flexible as well as portable electronic applications, as the need for miniaturized energy storage devices is in high demand.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The search for miniaturised energy storage devices with high energy density and power density led to the development of micro-supercapacitors (MSCs) [1]. The short lifetime and lack of power density led to the replacement of micro-batteries with MSCs. They provide high power density and long cycle life, particularly those with interdigitated structures [2]. The in-plane interdigitated MSCs fabricated by the Yuri Gogotsis group for the first time using carbide-derived carbon films (CDC) showed better performance than the conventional sandwich-like stacked devices [3]. The choice of electrode material also plays a crucial role in improving energy storage performance of MSCs. Transition metal oxides, conducting polymers and carbon-based materials are the commonly used electrode materials in MSCs, of which conducting polymer serves as a promising material in energy storage applications due to its high electrical conductivity, easy synthesis, tunability, flexibility and low cost [3]. Polyaniline with its unique characteristics and advantages is widely explored for energy storage applications. The major disadvantage of this material as a micro-supercapacitor is its low cycling stability due to the bulging of the material [4]. Several attempts were made to overcome this drawback, majorly based on the binary nanocomposites of polyaniline. Even though the nanocomposites of PANI with NiO, Fe2O3, and MnO2 [5–9] showed an increased capacitance, their applications restricted due to limited cycling stability. The binary nanocomposites of polyaniline with high active surface area carbonaceous material such as activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, carbon nanosheets and graphene have been extensively studied and found better stability and increased capacitance [9–14].

Carbon dots (CDs) are very high surface area carbon materials with good stability, strong absorptive power, and tunability that can act as an appropriate nanofiller in the polymer matrix [15]. CDs exhibit high electrical conductivity and possess numerous functional groups on their surface [16]. Heteroatoms doped CDs showed enhanced electrochemical properties making it a perfect nanofiller suitable for the conducting polymer matrix. Dually doped/co-doped CDs using nitrogen and sulphur showed higher performance than N doped/S doped CDs [17]. A recently published work developed a micro-supercapacitor using PANI/N doped CDs nanocomposite synthesised through oxidative polymerisation. Even though the cycling stability of PANI was improved (76.07 % for 5000 cycles), it showed an areal capacitance of only 128.01 mF cm-2 [18]. Electropolymerisation can be considered as the finest approach compared to different chemical methods as it offers reproducibility, cost-effectiveness, versatility, uniform coatings, and high purity [19–21]. One of the main advantages of this approach is that it allows tuning the properties of the deposited film under varying electrochemical conditions. Hence, replacing the oxidative polymerisation with electropolymerisation and the incorporation of N-S doped CDs instead of N doped into the PANI matrix can enhance the capacitance of the micro-supercapacitor. The electrodeposited polyaniline carbon dot nanocomposite is a less explored area, and to the best of our knowledge, this is the first ever reported flexible micro-supercapacitor using electrochemically synthesised polyaniline N-S doped carbon dot nanocomposite.

In this work, PANI/N-S doped nanocomposites were electrochemically synthesised and the capacitive behaviour of the nanocomposite was evaluated. The fabricated micro-supercapacitor using PANI/N-S doped binary nanocomposite showed increased areal capacitance and cycling stability than PANI.

Aniline (99 %), H2SO4 (98 %), ethylene diamine and citric acid were purchased from Finar Chemicals (India). Thiourea (98 %) was purchased from Loba Chemie. The preparation of the stock solutions was done with the help of Millipore deionized water (15.2 MΩ cm, Millipore, Germany). The carbon conducting inks and silver inks (32151190), were purchased from Siltech Corporation Inc. Ag/AgCl (5064H) was purchased from Dupont Pvt. Ltd. Singapore.

The electrochemical synthesis and testing were carried out using an electrochemical workstation (CHI6088D, CH Instruments, USA). The measurement of absorption was carried out using UV–Vis spectrometer (Pharmaspec 1700, Shimadzu, Japan) and fluorescence spectra using Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse Agilent, USA), respectively. The Raman spectral analysis was done at 532 nm using a Raman spectrometer (LabRAM evolution, HORIBA Jobin-Yvon, France). The morphology of the sample was analysed with the help of a Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM) (Gemini 300, Carl Zeiss, Germany) and High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) (JEM 2100, JEOL, JAPAN. The crystallinity and surface analysis were done using Grazing Incidence X-ray Diffraction (GIXRD) (Miniflex II, Rigaku, Japan) and XPS analysis using X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha, Thermofisher, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-S doped carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon dots was done with the help of hydrothermal method. The synthesis was initiated by the addition of 1 g citric acid and 0.4 M of EDA along with 0.45 g of thiourea into 15 mL of distilled water followed by continuous stirring. The above-prepared solution was kept in an autoclave at 180 °C for 8 h. N-S doped CDs were obtained at the end of the reaction, followed by centrifugation, syringe filtration (pore size 0.20 μm) and dialysis (MWCO = 1 KDa).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical synthesis of polyaniline nanocomposite (PANI/N-S CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Aniline solution under varying concentrations from 40 mM to 120 mM was prepared in 1 M H2SO4 solution. Electrodeposition of polyaniline was carried out for each of the concentrations in a three-electrode configuration using cyclic voltammetry on a platinum disc as the working electrode, platinum wire as the counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. The deposition was done for 20 cycles at a scan rate of 50 mV s-1 in a potential window of −0.2–1.2 V. After the optimisation of monomer concentration, N-S doped carbon dots in varying volumes (25, 50, 75 and 90 µL) were added to the optimised aniline solution followed by the electrodeposition.

Initially, the PET sheets were cleaned with acetone, and it was cured at 100 °C for 1 h. For the printing of interdigitated electrodes that had a half area of about 1.865 cm2, silver ink was used which acted as the conducting layer and on top of that conductive carbon ink was coated. The printed electrodes were cured at 90 °C for 30 min after the printing of each layer [22] . After this, optimised PANI and PANI/N-S doped carbon dot nanocomposite were electrodeposited using cyclic voltammtery on the screen-printed electrode under a potential window of −0.2–1.2 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s-1. The deposited film was then evenly coated with PVA-H2SO4 gel electrolyte. For preparing the gel electrolyte, 1 g of PVA was added to 25 mL of deionised water with continuous stirring for 2 h at 90 °C and cooled down to room temperature followed by the addition of 15 mL of H2SO4, making the effective concentration of the gel electrolyte to 1 M [22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopic characterisation of N-S doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of nitrogen and sulphur co-doped CDs were analysed using UV–Vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy and the results are presented in Fig. S1. The absorption bands observed at 245 nm and 340 nm for N-S doped carbon dots are due to the π-π* transitions in the aromatic domains of the carbon core and the n-π* transition of C=O bonding, respectively [23,24]. N-S doped CDs showed excellent fluorescence emission under UV light (Fig. S1 inset). The quantum efficiency of N-S doped CDs was calculated using equation (1) given in the supplementary material in section S1 by taking quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as the reference. Interestingly, they showed a high quantum yield of 70.02 %, which is due to the introduction of sulphur atoms that might have led to the increase in electron density of N states through a cooperative effect [24].

The XRD measurement of the synthesised N-S doped CDs was carried out and shown in Fig. S2. The amorphous nature of the synthesised CDs was confirmed by the characteristic peak observed at 24.6º corresponding to (002) plane of graphitic carbon [25]. The Raman spectrum of the synthesised N-S doped CDs showed the presence of D band at 1376 cm-1 representing the sp3 hybridised carbon atoms and disorder of the system and the G band around 1593 cm-1 representing the sp2 hybridised carbon (Fig. S3). The observed shifts for the D and G band towards higher wavelengths can be attributed to the co-doping of nitrogen and sulphur [26,27]. The higher intensity of the D band compared to that of the G band gave a higher ID/IG ratio, showing a higher degree of disorder and defects, confirming the formation of an amorphous system. The crystalline size of the N-S doped CDs was calculated using the modified form of the Tuinstra-Koenig equation provided in the supplementary information in section S1 (equation 2) and found to be 3.5 nm [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterisation of PANI/N-S doped nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The electropolymerisation initiates with the formation of anilinium cation radicals, followed by further growth of the polymer. The 1st potential cycle in the electropolymerisation shows an anodic peak at around 1.2 V vs Ag/AgCl (Fig. S4A) due to the oxidation of anilinium ion which is the acid-soluble form of aniline [29]. The absence of reduction peaks suggests that the first step is irreversible. In the subsequent scans, more faradaic peaks were obtained and were numbered in the figure. Peak 1 corresponds to the oxidation of leucoemeraldine to emeraldine and its reduction is represented as Peak 1ʹ. Peaks 2 and 2ʹ correspond to the oxidation of hydroquinone to benzoquinone and the reversible reduction reaction of polyaniline degradation products, respectively. Peaks 3 and 3ʹ represent the reversible redox reaction between emeraldine and pernigraniline [29]. Fig. S4B shows the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of the electrodeposited PANI, obtained with different monomeric concentrations (40–100 mM), in 1 M H2SO4. An increase in areal capacitance was observed with an increase in monomer concentration, and the calculated areal capacitances are compared in the bar diagram (Fig. S4B inset). It was found that the areal capacitance was steeply increased at lower concentrations, and a gradual increase was observed after 80 mM monomer solution. The increased areal capacitance can be attributed to the increased surface area and the increased mass loading at higher concentrations. Thus, the aniline concentration of 100 mM was selected for further investigations. The incorporation of N-S doped CDs in polyaniline resulted in a considerable increase in current during the potentiodynamic polymerisation of aniline (Fig. S4C and D).

The effect of scan rate on the electrochemical performance of the electrodeposited PANI film tested in 1 M H2SO4 are represented in Fig. 1 A. The shifting of anodic peaks to the positive region and the cathodic peaks to the negative region upon the increase in the scan rate must be due to the limited availability of ions at the electrode surface [18]. It was also observed that with the increase in the scan rate, the peaks broadened, which can be attributed to the reduction in the size of the diffusion layer with the increase in the scan rate [30]. Fig. 1B shows the CVs obtained after the addition of N-S doped CDs in varying volumes (25, 50, 75 and 90 µL). The nanocomposite obtained with 50 µL of N-S doped CDs showed the highest areal capacitance and further increase in composition of CDs decreased the areal capacitance, maintaining the same pseudocapacitive behaviour (Fig. 1B inset). The decrease in the capacitance on increasing the composition of CDs may be due to the agglomeration of particles, which results in a decreased surface area [23]. Hence, PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite obtained with 50 µL N-S doped CDs was used for further studies. The areal capacitance calculated for PANI and the optimised PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite from the CVs was found to be 754 mF cm-2 and 990 mF cm-2 respectively. As with the PANI modified electrode, cyclic voltammetry was performed with PANI/N-S CDs doped nanocomposite at various scan rates (Fig. 1C) and found that the oxidation and reduction peaks are more prominent in the voltammogram of the composite. This can be attributed to the formation of a stable structure in which the carbon dots are uniformly distributed into the polymer matrix, through the p-p interaction and the hydrogen bonding [23]. The comparison of the areal capacitance of the PANI and the composite (Fig. 1D) shows that the latter exhibited higher areal capacitance at all scan rates. The involvement of sulphur and nitrogen in N-S doped carbon dots increases the density of active sites that can trap more ions from the electrolyte can be stated as the reason for the increased areal capacitance of the composite [31]. The galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) analysis at different current densities in Fig. 1E showed almost linear charge-discharge profiles that portray the pseudocapacitive behaviour of the deposited nanocomposite. The areal capacitance calculated from the GCD curves showed a maximum areal capacitance of 481 mF cm-2 at a current density of 12.5 mA cm-2. The impedance spectrum (Fig. 1F) of the PANI and the nanocomposite was fitted in the equivalent circuit R(QR)(Q) and determined the Rct. The PANI exhibited an Rct of 271.6 Ω whereas the nanocomposite showed a much lower Rct of 45.44 Ω, which accounts for the increased surface area and conductivity of the PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite. Rs values observed for PANI and PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite were 6.4 and 7.3 Ω, respectively. The Bode plot (Fig. 1F inset) shows a considerable increase in the phase angle from 71.8 to 84.7 for PANI and PANI/N-S at a lower frequency of 0.008 Hz. The knee frequency obtained was 0.2 Hz for the nanocomposite, which was about 7 times higher compared to that obtained for PANI (0.03 Hz). A noticeable increase in both the phase angle and the knee frequency for the nanocomposite accounts for its excellent capacitive behaviour compared to pure PANI.

The difference in the molar concentration of the electrolyte used for testing has a significant impact on the electrochemical activity. Therefore, the nanocomposite modified electrode was tested at different molar concentrations of H2SO4 and the resulting CVs are shown in Fig. S5A. The highest areal capacitance (Fig. S5B) was observed in 1 M H2SO4 and further increase in acid concentration decreased the electrochemical activity [32]. Hence, 1 M H2SO4 was fixed as the optimum concentration of H2SO4 required for further testing and analysis.

The charge storage mechanism in PANI and the nanocomposite was evaluated using the Dunn method, and the capacitive current contribution was determined using equation (4) provided in the supplementary material (section S3). The results showed a trend of increasing current with the increase in scan rate for both PANI and the nanocomposite. A maximum capacitive contribution of 80 % was obtained for PANI at a higher scan rate of 100 mV s-1, whereas it increased to 91 % for the nanocomposite (Fig. 2 A, B). At the lowest scan rate (2 mV s-1), a considerable increase (21 %) in the capacitive behaviour for the nanocomposite compared to pure PANI, comments on the capacitive property of N-S doped CDs . A linear increase was obtained by plotting log peak current vs. log scan rate, where a slope of 0.85 was observed for both PANI and the nanocomposite, indicates their superior capacitive behaviour (Fig. 2C and D).

The TEM imaging confirmed the formation of carbon dots, quasi-spherical in shape, as shown in Fig. 3 A. The particle size calculated using the Gaussian fitting functions reveals the presence of particles with diameters ranging from 2.5 to 6.5 nm, in which the average particle size of approximately 100 particles was estimated as 4.1 nm (Fig. 3B). The lattice spacing was observed to be 0.21 nm, confirming the formation of a typical graphitic structure as represented in Fig. 3A inset [15]. The uniform and narrow size distribution of the carbon dots obtained by controlled synthesis conditions helps in their regular arrangement during the formation of electrodeposited PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite.

FE-SEM image of the electrodeposited PANI (Fig. 3C) shows highly entangled nano-fibril structures. The addition of N-S doped carbon dots made the nano-fibrils more clustered with a considerable reduction in their diameter, as shown in Fig. 3D. This accounts for the incorporation of carbon dots into the polymer matrix that led to the aggregation of the polymer. The morphology of the nanocomposite helps in the percolation process, making the doping and de-doping of the electrolyte ions much easier [33].

The XRD pattern of PANI, and PANI/N-S doped CDs binary composite is shown in Fig. 4 A. The peaks observed for PANI and PANI/N-S CDs nanocomposite at 2θ=26.28º with a shoulder peak at 22.1º, correspond to the (200) and (020) (hkl) lattice planes [34]. Similarly, the peak at 44.4º corresponds to the (101) lattice plane of carbon which is also observed for both the electrodeposited films [35].

The Raman spectroscopic analysis for PANI and PANI/N-S doped CDs binary nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 4B The intensity of peaks for the nanocomposite was increased compared with PANI, which might be due to the well-ordered arrangement of molecules by the addition of N-S doped carbon dots. The peak at 1592.4 cm-1 corresponds to the stretching of carbon-carbon double bonds in quinine rings [36]. The peak at 1330 cm-1 attributed to the stretching vibrations of the intermediate bond CN+ [37]. The respective peak at 1166 cm-1 corresponds to the bending vibration of CH in benzene and quinoid rings [38]. In addition to this, an additional peak was observed for PANI/N-S doped carbon dots nanocomposite at 1404 cm-1, which is due to the disordered carbon [39].

The survey spectrum recorded for PANI/N-S CDs binary nanocomposite shows the presence of C1s, N1s, O1s, and S2p at 284.87, 399.3, 531.32, and 168.23 eV, respectively (Fig. S6) [23]. The atomic percentages of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and sulphur were obtained 75.87 %, 11.29 %, 10.44 %, and 2.4 %, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum for O1s is shown in Fig. 4C, where the deconvoluted peaks appearing at the binding energies 531.1 and 530.2 eV, corresponds to the C=O group and 532.1 eV to the COH (hydroxyl) and COC (ether) [23,40]. The deconvoluted peaks at 283.9 eV and 283.3 eV correspond to sp2 hybridised C=C carbon (Fig. 4D). The remaining peaks at 285.9 eV and 284.7 eV correspond to CN/CO and sp3 hybridised CH carbon respectively [23]. Similarly, for N1s as shown in Fig. 4E, three deconvoluted peaks were observed at 400.2 eV, 399.2 eV, and 398.4 eV representing pyrrolic nitrogen/pyridone nitrogen in association with oxygen functionality (N-5), amine (-NH-) groups and pyridinic nitrogen (N-6) [40]. The deconvoluted peaks for S2p (Fig. 4F) were observed at 168.7, 167.7, and 167 eV which represents the S=O designating the interconnection of S with the polymer matrix [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing of two-electrode assembly symmetric supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: The electropolymerisation of PANI/N-S CDs nanocomposite on the interdigitated electrode was carried out, tested in 1 M H2SO4, and the results are presented in Fig. S7. An increase in current was observed with an increase in potential cycles of electropolymerisation. The peaks observed at a potential range of 0.1–0.4 V and 0.5–0.8 V correspond to the transformation of leucoemeraldine to the emeraldine state and the formation of p-benzoquinone, followed by their oxidation and reduction [41]. The comparison of the CVs for electrodeposited PANI and PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite-based supercapacitors tested in 1 M PVA/H2SO4 is represented in Fig. 5 A. A high areal capacitance of 350 mF cm-2 was observed for the nanocomposite-based supercapacitor, whereas pristine PANI-based system showed only 267.7 mF cm-2 at 2 mV s-1. The addition of carbon dots has increased the electroactive sites for electrochemical reactions to occur, thereby giving a high areal capacitance. The GCD curves (Fig. 5B) showed a decrease in IR drop for the nanocomposite-based supercapacitor compared to the PANI-based system, which points out the increase in the area of the complex after the addition of carbon dots, resulting in decreased resistance [42]. This shows excellent capacitive performance of the fabricated micro-supercapacitor using PANI/N-S doped CDs nanocomposite. The cyclic voltammetric measurements carried out at different scan rates for the fabricated micro-supercapacitor are presented in Fig. 5C. The oxidation and reduction peaks that represent the transitions between the leucoemeraldine-emeraldine and emeraldine-pernigraniline states are not so visible, and the pseudocapacitive behaviour is not profound at higher scan rates. This can be due to the less residence time for a perfect interaction of the ions in the gel electrolyte with the polymer matrix. At a lower scan rate, the electrolyte ions and the ions in the polymer matrix get enough interaction time, thereby increasing the probability of the redox reaction leading to more prominent peaks. Also, a shift in oxidation and reduction peaks to the more positive and negative regions is observed at higher scan rates which is due to the lack of availability of ions at the electrode-electrolyte interface [18]. The areal capacitance kept increasing with the decrease in scan rate for the fabricated micro-supercapacitor as shown in Fig. 5D, and a maximum areal capacitance of 350 mF cm-2 at 2 mV s-1 was obtained. The GCD profiles (Fig. 5E) of the fabricated micro-supercapacitor performed at different current densities showed almost triangular shape, having a nonlinear charge-discharge profile, which accounts for excellent pseudocapacitive charge storage in addition to the EDLC behaviour [23]. An areal capacitance of 276.9 mF cm-2 and specific capacitance of 134.26 Fg-1 were obtained at a current density of 0.4 mA cm-2. Under this condition, a high areal energy density of 24.6 µWh cm-2 and specific energy density of 11.934 Wh kg-1, and areal power density of 0.17 mW cm-2 with a specific power density of 82.88 W kg-1 were obtained. The Nyquist plot for the fabricated micro-supercapacitor (Fig. 5F) matched with the same equivalent circuit R(QR)(QR) as done for the three-electrode system. The lower charge transfer resistance and solution resistance (11 Ω and 11.9 Ω) are due to the high conductivity of the N-S doped CDs.

A comparison study of energy density and its corresponding power densities of the present work with other recently reported works of micro-supercapacitors is represented with the help of Ragone plot as shown in Fig. 6 A. The maximum energy density and power density that reached 24.6 µWh cm-2 and 0.17 mW cm-2 is superior to most of the reported works. The cycling stability test for the PANI as well as PANI/N-S doped CDs binary nanocomposite is represented in Fig. 6B The composite material was allowed to charge galvanostatically and discharge under a constant current density of about 1.6 mA cm-2. It is clear from the data that PANI has a drastic decrease in the capacitance after 850 cycles and retains a capacitance of 51.29 % after 1500 cycles. In the case of N-S doped CDs nanocomposite, it showed a capacitance retention of about 98.49 % even after 15000 cycles. It was found that the capacitance decreased initially and then increased and maintained a relatively constant capacitance after 12000 cycles. The increase in the capacitance can be due to the involvement of the activation mechanism by the diffusion of electrolyte into the electrode material, resulting in the enhanced wettability [43].

The fabricated micro-supercapacitor tested at different bending angles gave a capacitance retention of 93.3 % even after 2000 cycles (Fig. 6C). An increase in areal capacitance was observed after 300 cycles which can be explained on the basis of mechanical-electrochemical synergy activation [22]. The electrolyte ions percolate into the electrode material when subjected to continuous bending cycles. The flexibility test performed shows the potential of the device for flexible electronics.

The series and parallel combinations of two and three cells of the fabricated micro-supercapacitors shown in Fig. 6D and E showed increased potential window and current, respectively. The series combination with three cells was enough for lighting red LED (20 mA, 2 V) and a scientific calculator (Casio fx 82-MS,1.5 V, 13–23 µA as shown in Fig. 6F.

A comparison study of this work with other recently reported works on micro-supercapacitors using polyaniline-based nanocomposite is shown in Table S1. The areal capacitance and cycling stability are higher than most of the reported works. In addition to this, the fabricated device possesses excellent flexibility compared to other reported works. Interestingly, none of the reported works have fabricated micro-supercapacitors using electrochemically synthesised polyaniline/N-S doped nanocomposite. Hence, this is the first reported electrodeposited PANI/N-S CDs fabricated flexible micro-supercapacitor with high areal capacitance and excellent cycling stability.

The study focused on the development of a new method towards the synthesis of polyaniline carbon dot nanocomposite. The fabricated device that involved the electrochemically synthesised PANI/N-S doped CDs as the electrode material showed a very high capacitance of 276.9 mF cm-2 at a current density of 0.4 mA cm-2 and excellent cycling stability of 15000 cycles with 98.49 % capacitance retention. In addition to this, the flexible nature of the fabricated device was confirmed by the flexibility test that gave 93.3 % capacitance retention after 2000 cycles. The easy as well as tunable method of synthesis and high cycling stability, complemented with the flexibility, finds the application of the device towards wearable and flexible devices.

M Architha: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. P K Neena: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Punnakkal Navaneeth: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Investigation. T G Satheesh Babu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Punathil Vasu Suneesh: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India, through the project (DST/CEST/AMEST/2022/55). The authors express their sincere thanks to Amrita School of Physical Sciences, Amrita Vishwa Vidyapeetham for Raman spectrophotometer facility and SAIF, CUSAT for TEM facility.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2025.146518.
